1: A revelation of God: "Let everyone be subject to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except that which God has established." The divine institution of governing authority grounds political submission in theology, not merely pragmatism. The same God who raised Christ is the one who establishes authorities (Daniel 2:21); submission to them is indirect submission to the divine ordering of creation.
2: A revelation of God: "Whoever rebels against the authority is rebelling against what God has instituted, and those who do so will bring judgment on themselves." The theological weight of political order is grounded in divine institution, not in the virtue of the rulers. This does not imply unlimited obedience (Acts 5:29 "we must obey God rather than human beings") but baseline respect for ordered authority.
3: A theme: "Rulers hold no terror for those who do right, but for those who do wrong." The governing authority as servant of good and executor of judgment on evil is the creational role that participates in the divine moral order. The Christ who is Lord over all governing powers (Romans 8:38-39; Colossians 2:15) delegated limited authority to human governments to maintain order in the present age.
4: A revelation of God: The governing authority is "God's servant" (diakonos theou) for good, and "God's servant" as an agent of wrath against the wrongdoer. The same diakonos vocabulary used for Christ (Romans 15:8) and the apostolic community (Romans 12:7) is applied to the governing authority — indicating that all legitimate service participates in the same divine ordering of reality toward good.
5: A theme: Submission is "not only because of possible punishment but also as a matter of conscience." The conscience-argument lifts political submission above mere fear into the domain of the renewed mind (Romans 12:2). The conscience renewed by the Spirit (cf. Romans 2:15; Romans 9:1) perceives the legitimacy of God's ordering of political life and responds accordingly.
6: A revelation of God: "This is also why you pay taxes, for the authorities are God's servants, who give their full time to governing." Taxation is a corollary of recognizing governing authorities as divinely sanctioned servants; supporting them financially is part of the community's engagement with the ordered social world in which the gospel is proclaimed.
7: A theme: "Give to everyone what you owe them: If you owe taxes, pay taxes; if revenue, then revenue; if respect, then respect; if honor, then honor." The comprehensive rendering of what is owed establishes the community as conscientious participants in social order rather than subversives. The same Christ who said "give to Caesar what is Caesar's" (Matthew 22:21) is the Lord whose instructions these are.
8: A direct: "Let no debt remain outstanding, except the continuing debt to love one another, for whoever loves others has fulfilled the law." The love-debt is the one debt that can never be fully discharged because the object of love (the neighbor) always presents new occasion. The love is the same love that Christ demonstrated (Romans 5:8) and that the Spirit pours into believers' hearts (Romans 5:5), now expressed toward neighbors.
9: A direct: "The commandments... are summed up in this one command: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.'" The summary of the law in the love-commandment is what Christ declared the second greatest commandment (Matthew 22:39) and what he modeled by dying for enemies. Love as law-fulfillment is not the abolition of the commandments but their distillation into their deepest intent.
10: A direct: "Love does no harm to a neighbor. Therefore love is the fulfillment of the law." Love's negative definition — not harming the neighbor — corresponds to the law's own negative structure (the Ten Commandments are mostly prohibitions). Christ, who is the telos of the law (Romans 10:4), is also the supreme embodiment of love (Romans 5:8); in him law and love are not in tension but united.
11: A direct: "And do this, understanding the present time: The hour has already come for you to wake up from your slumber, because our salvation is nearer now than when we first believed." The eschatological urgency is grounded in the resurrection of Christ, which established the "now" of the overlap between the ages. The salvation drawing near is the completion of what Christ's resurrection inaugurated.
12: A direct: "The night is nearly over; the day is almost here. So let us put aside the deeds of darkness and put on the armor of light." The "armor of light" is specifically named in Romans 13:14 as Christ himself; the metaphorical armor is the person of the risen Lord. The coming day is the day of his return; the present night is the interim that his resurrection established.
13: A direct: "Let us behave properly, as in the day" — the ethical behavior Paul lists (not in carousing, debauchery, strife, jealousy) is the conduct appropriate to those who already belong to the day-of-resurrection. They live in the present night as those who bear the resurrection's character, since they have been raised with Christ (Romans 6:4).
14: A direct: "Rather, clothe yourselves with the Lord Jesus Christ, and do not think about how to gratify the desires of the flesh." The climactic ethical imperative of the chapter names Christ himself as the believer's clothing — the identity to be put on and worn. The same verb-and-noun complex (cf. Galatians 3:27 "all who were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ") identifies the clothing with the person of the Lord. Putting on Christ is not imitation but participation.
